source_metadata.title_raw: Rebecca L. Moon - A-19-236 - January 22, 2020 - Sunnyvale

content.full_text:
• 
Rebecca L. Moon 
Senior Assistant City Attorney 
456 West Olive Avenue 
Sunnyvale, CA 94088-3707 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-236 
Dear Ms. Moon: 
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886
January 22, 2020
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Sunnyvale Mayor Larry 
Klein, Councilmember Gustav Larsson, and Planning Commissioner Ken Rheaume regarding the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTIONS 
1. May Mayor Klein participate in decisions regarding amendments to the Downtown
Specific Plan ("DSP") where his residence is located within 640 feet of the DSP boundary? 
2. Following the City's decision to amend the DSP, may Councilmember Larsson, Planning
Commissioner Rheaume, or Mayor Klein participate in the decisions relating to the six specific 
development projects within the DSP? 
CONCLUSIONS 
1. No. It is reasonably foreseeable the decisions to amend the DSP will have a material
financial effect on the market value of Mayor Klein's residence under Regulation 18702.2(a)(8), 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-19-236 
Page No. 2 
due to the proposed sizable changes in the density, height, and type of use in the DSP 
developments. 
2. Where the officials have a conflict of interest in the DSP amendment decisions, each 
official may only participate in the six specific project decisions to the extent that the official does 
not otherwise have a conflict of interest in the decision, the decision is properly segmented under 
Regulation 18706, and the decision will not in effect reopen, determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the 
DSP amendment decisions. However, until the adoption of the DSP amendment it would premature 
to consider the official's participation the specific project decisions as we cannot identify the exact 
nature of any decision at this time. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
Mayor Larry Klein, Councilmember Gustav Larsson, and Planning Commissioner Ken 
Rheaume each own a single-family home in the residential neighborhood immediately south of the 
City's Downtown Specific Plan ("DSP") area. The City adopted the existing the DSP in 1993, 
revised it in 2003 and most recently amended it in 2013 to guide planning and redevelopment in the 
downtown area. Currently, due to six development project proposals for sites within the DSP, the 
City is considering amendments to the DSP that will allow for higher density, a shift from retail 
spaces to residential and office (mixed-use) spaces, and taller buildings in the Commercial Core 
District of the DSP, as well as additional policy and goal changes. 
Background on the DSP 
The DSP area is roughly 125 acres bounded by Charles Street to the west, Evelyn Avenue to 
the north, Bayview A venue to the east, Olive A venue to the south, and with a narrow extension 
south along Mathilda A venue to El Camino Real. The DSP is divided into blocks 1 to 23. The DSP 
blocks are also grouped into four districts, each of which has its own set of development standards. 
These are the Commercial Core District; South of Iowa District with a lower-density residential 
neighborhood intended to buffer the single-family residential neighborhood south of Iowa Avenue 
from the Commercial Core; and the West of Matilda and Sunnyvale/Carroll Districts with higher-
density residential neighborhoods. 
All six of the proposed specific development projects are in the Commercial Core. Four of 
the proposed development project sites are in the Town Center (Block 18).2 The other two specific 
project proposals are north of the Town Center, in Blocks 1, la and 22 in the Commercial Core.3 
Full buildout of all six sites under the existing DSP would result in a total of 93 residential units, 
181,000 square feet of commercial uses, 17,896 square feet of office uses, and 200 hotel rooms. All 
2 This area is divided into sub-blocks numbered 1 to 6. The Town Center (Block 18) was a large enclosed mall 
slated as early as 2007 for replacement by residential units, office space, a hotel, public plaza and retail. Due to the 
financial crisis, the area was in limbo with unfinished projects from 2008 until 2016. In 2016, the "City line" developer 
completed three 5-story mixed use apartment buildings on Block 18 and began constructing a 2-story retail building for 
Whole Foods and an AMC theater. Cityline removed some of the earlier abandoned partial construction and completed 
streetscape improvements throughout the Town Center (Block 18). 
3 Blocks 1 and la currently have a 1 story commercial building and a 3-story, 20-unit apartment building. The 
two sites are ringed by new development of 5- and 6-story office and mixed-use apartment buildings. Block 22 is a 
surface parking lot near the Caltrain station and serves a 2-4 story office/commercial center. 


File No. A-19-236 
Page No. 3 
the project sites are already developed with office, commercial, residential uses, and/or surface 
parking except for Block 18, Building B, 300 Mathilda Avenue, which is currently undeveloped. 
The current development proposals for these sites and proposed amendments to the DSP reflect the 
decline in demand for retail and commercial space in the past decade, and the high demand for 
dense, mixed-use transit-oriented development in the area. 
Proposed DSP Amendments 
The owners of the six proposed projects requested amendments to the DSP to change 
the development regulations affecting their sites; however, City staff decided to take the 
opportunity to also make global updates to some of the DSP's general goals, policies, 
and parking standards. If the City Council approves the DSP amendments, the specific development 
projects will be brought to the Planning Commission and/or the City Council for review and 
approval at later dates. 
To summarize, the proposed DSP amendments would allow for the development of a total 
of 843 residential units (an increase of 750 units compared to the existing DSP), 260,063 square 
feet of commercial uses (an increase of79,063 square feet compared to the existing DSP), 860,624 
square feet of office uses (an increase of 842,728 square feet compared to the existing DSP), and no 
hotel rooms (a decrease of 200 hotel rooms compared to the existing DSP). The proposed 
amendments would allow for higher density, a shift from retail spaces to residential and office 
(mixed-use) spaces, and taller buildings in the current Commercial Core. The amendments also call 
for a community benefits program related to affordable housing, transportation improvements, 
publicly-accessible open space, or contributions toward public amenities or services as a part of the 
changes in proposed building heights and density. 
Following the decision on the amendments, the City will consider addition decisions 
regarding the six specific development projects. You indicate that the specific project decisions may 
largely relate to the development agreements with the developers within the parameters of the DSP 
and may include specific architectural and community benefits elements. 
Financial Interests: Real Property 
All three officials own a residential, single-family home in the area south of the DSP. 
Mayor Klein owns and resides in a single-family home approximately 640 feet south of the 
DSP boundary, 718 feet east of the nearest point on the Commercial Core (Block 20), 1,367 feet 
south of the Town Center (Block 18), and 1,510 feet from the nearest proposed development on 
Block 18, Sub-block 1. 
Councilmember Larsson owns and resides in a single-family home approximately 169 feet 
from southeast corner the DSP boundary (the corner of Iowa Ave. and Sunnyvale Ave.), 863 feet 
south of the Town Center (Block 18) (which is also the nearest point on the Commercial Core), and 
1,357 feet from the nearest proposed development site in the Town Center (Block 18, Sub-block 6). 


File No. A-19-236 
Page No. 4 
Commissioner Rheaume owns and resides in a single-family home next door to 
Councilmember Larsson. His home is a few feet farther away from the DSP, with a similar distance 
from each of the proposed projects. 
Environmental Impacts: The DSP amendments and the Six Specific Projects 
The City's Draft Environmental Impact Report ("EIR") to analyze the effects of the DSP 
amendments and the six specific development project proposals found that it would contribute to 
long-term worsening traffic congestion in the surrounding area. To summarize, the EIR identified 
several significant and unavoidable cumulative traffic impacts outside the DSP at intersections 
within a one to three-mile distance from the officials' residences, and a regionally significant and 
unavoidable impact on Highway 23 7 during peak commute hours in the area. The amendments to 
the DSP propose to reopen Frances Avenue, however, there are permanent traffic barriers on South 
Frances Avenue that will protect Mayor Klein's home on Frances Avenue from cut-through traffic 
between the Town Center and El Camino Real. 
With regard to Mayor Klein's residence, you state that no changes are proposed that would 
alter Mayor Klein's parcel's zoning, aesthetics, use, develop capacity, or heritage district 
protections. You note that the EIR does not identify any significant environmental impacts on the 
residents south of Olive Avenue. Based on a City parking study, there does not appear to be any 
indication of spillover parking into the neighborhood south of Olive. Also, a Traffic Impact 
Analysis indicates that the DSP amendments and the six specific development projects will have 
minimal impact on traffic within the south of Olive neighborhood, even under cumulative 
conditions. Finally, the improvements to streetscapes in the Town Center (Block 18) have been 
completed and the proposed amendments do not anticipate any further enhancements to that area. 
You note that the EIR, Traffic Impact Analysis, and Parking Study indicate that the projects 
will have no significant effects on the officials' parcels related to intensity of use, parking, view, 
privacy, noise levels, air quality, or traffic. The officials' homes are more than 1,000 feet from the 
closest development sites and buffered from the development proposed on Block 18 by the South of 
Iowa residential neighborhood, as well as the previously entitled projects that are being built on 
Block 18 facing Iowa Avenue (i.e. the Whole Foods, AMC theater, and the approved townhomes 
and apartments on Iowa). The development sites on Blocks 1 and la and Block 22 are even farther 
away from the officials' homes and are separated from them by the South of Iowa neighborhood, 
the entire Town Center, the multistory apartment buildings on Washington Avenue and the block of 
historic shops and restaurants on Murphy A venue. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit any public official from making, 
participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental 
decision in which the official has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) A public official has a 
"financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on one or more of the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 
18700(a).) Section 87103 defines a financial interest to include any real property in which the 


File No. A-19-236 
Page No. 5 
public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. Each of the officials has a real 
property interest in their respective residential homes located adjacent to the DSP 
DSP Amendment Decisions 
Mayor Klein 
Where the financial interest is not explicitly involved in a decision, the financial effect is 
reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility, more than hypothetical or 
theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).) Mayor Klein owns real property outside the DSP boundaries, 
and his real property is not the subject of the DSP amendment decisions. Under Regulation 
18701 (b ), he will have a financial interest in the DSP amendment decisions if it is a realistic 
possibility the decisions will have a material financial effect on his real property interest. 
Mayor Klein's home is within 640 feet of the DSP boundary. Regulation 18702.2(a)(8), 
regarding real property within 500 to 1,000 feet from the property involved in a decision, is the 
applicable standard. Under Regulation 18702.2(a)(8), a decision's effect on an official's real 
property interest is material if the decision would change the parcel's market value, development 
potential, income producing potential, highest and best use, or character by substantially altering 
traffic levels, intensity of use, parking, view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality. 
The proposed DSP amendments would allow for an increase of 750 residential units, an 
increase of79,063 square feet commercial space, an increase of 842,728 square feet office space 
and a decrease of 200 hotel rooms compared to the existing DSP to facilitate the six specific 
development projects. A community benefits program is partnered with these changes, and intended 
to enhance public amenities, housing and services. With the loss of demand for retail and 
commercial development, it is unlikely that the developments previously anticipated under the 
current DSP would occur. 
Based on these facts, it is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed DSP amendments will 
have a material financial effect on his residence. It is reasonably foreseeable that the increase in 
density of use, and the change from retail to a mixed residential and office use, will impact the 
market value of Mayor Klein's home. In past advice we have concluded that projects much smaller 
in scale involving land not currently in residential use would impact the market value of homes 
located within the 500 to 1,00 feet distance. (See, e.g., Chopra Advice Letter, No. A-18-098 
[reasonably foreseeable that construction of 118 new residential dwellings on land utilized as a 
public park located about 930 feet from residences of two City of Mountain View councilmembers 
would have a material financial effect on the market value of their homes; Wright Advice Letter, 
No. A-17-201 [reasonably foreseeable that mixed-use development that includes 10.45 acres of 
commercial use, 98 condominiums, 22 single family detached homes on undeveloped land 700 feet 
from Mayor's Antioch residence would have a material financial effect on the market value of 
surrounding properties, even those 700 feet away].) Therefore, decisions concerning amendments to 
the DSP will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on the Mayor's real 
property interest, and the Mayor should not take part in the decisions. 


Councilmember Larsson and Commissioner Rheaume 
File No. A-19-236 
Page No. 6 
Councilmember Larsson and Commissioner Rheaume both live within 500 feet of the DSP. 
Under Regulation 18702.2(a)(7), the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a decision on an 
interest in real property is material whenever the decision "[i]nvolves property located 500 feet or 
less from the property line of the parcel unless there is clear and convincing evidence that the 
decision will not have any measurable impact on the official's property." Accordingly, both 
Councilmember Larson and Commissioner Rheaume are prohibited from taking part in decisions 
concerning amendments to the DSP. As presented in your request, this conflict of interest for the 
two officials in the DSP amendment decisions is understood, and not at issue in the request. 
Specific Project Decisions 
Your second question concerns whether the officials may participate in later decisions 
relating to the six proposed development projects. 
The Act's segmentation rules allow that an official with a conflict of interest may participate 
in decisions that do not in effect reopen or affect the decision in which the official has a conflict of 
interest. Regulation 18706 allows for some large, complex decisions to be segmented into separate 
decisions so that even if an official has a disqualifying interest in one component of the series of 
decisions, he or she may still participate in other components in which there is no financial interest. 
Regulation 18706 requires the following: 
(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down 
into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision in 
which the official has a disqualifying financial interest; 
(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from 
the other decisions; 
(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first 
and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official's 
participation in any way; and 
(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made, 
the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening of, or 
otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official was 
disqualified." 
Where a decision in which an official has a conflict of interest is "inextricably interrelated" 
(the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another 
decision), the official will be required to disqualify from both decisions. (Regulation 18706(b).) 
We have previously advised the City of Sunnyvale that DSP-related decisions that 
implement the DSP, the development plan for Block 18, or the earlier Town Center Project could be 
segmented to allow an official's participation so long as the decisions do not reopen, determine, 


File No. A-19-236 
Page No. 7 
affirm, nullify, or alter the previous decisions approving the DSP. However, we have also advised 
that decisions involving major policy decisions or decisions that may determine whether aspects of 
the DSP will move forward are inextricably interrelated to the decisions in which he has a conflict 
of interest and may not be segmented to allow for his participation.4 
You state that the DSP amendments will be considered first, and then, once the amendments 
are approved, the six specific project decisions will be considered. However, we do not have 
specific decisions to analyze currently. 
As a general matter, we advise that Mayor Klein, Councilmember Larsson and 
Commissioner Rheaume may participate in the specific project decisions to the extent that they do 
not otherwise have a financial interest in the specific project decisions, and the decisions are 
properly segmented from the final DSP amendment decisions under Regulation 18706. Decisions 
that will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of the DSP amendment decisions 
will be inextricably interrelated to the DSP amendment decisions and may not be segmented to 
allow for the official's participation. However, as the nature of any specific decision regarding the 
six specific projects cannot be fully determined prior to the adoption of the DSP amendment, 
considering whether the decisions could be segmented at this time would be premature and purely 
hypothetical. Accordingly, you may wish to seek additional assistance regarding the specific project 
decisions in the event the DSP amendment is adopted. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
LKH:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
t:r-k 
L. Karen Harrison 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division 
4 See Borger Advice Letter, No. 1-15-110, Borger Advice Letter, No. A-15-226, and Nagel Advice Letter, No. 
A-16-101.

Commissioner Rheaume, or Mayor Klein participate in the decisions relating to the six specific 
development projects within the DSP?

financial effect on the market value of Mayor Klein's residence under Regulation 18702.2(a)(8),due to the proposed sizable changes in the density, height, and type of use in the DSP 
developments. 
2. Where the officials have a conflict of interest in the DSP amendment decisions, each 
official may only participate in the six specific project decisions to the extent that the official does

DSP amendment decisions. However, until the adoption of the DSP amendment it would premature

nature of any decision at this time.

Your second question concerns whether the officials may participate in later decisions 
relating to the six proposed development projects. 
The Act's segmentation rules allow that an official with a conflict of interest may participate 
in decisions that do not in effect reopen or affect the decision in which the official has a conflict of 
interest. Regulation 18706 allows for some large, complex decisions to be segmented into separate 
decisions so that even if an official has a disqualifying interest in one component of the series of 
decisions, he or she may still participate in other components in which there is no financial interest. 
Regulation 18706 requires the following: 
(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down 
into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision in 
which the official has a disqualifying financial interest; 
(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from 
the other decisions; 
(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first 
and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official's 
participation in any way; and 
(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made, 
the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening of, or 
otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official was 
disqualified." 
Where a decision in which an official has a conflict of interest is "inextricably interrelated" 
(the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another 
decision), the official will be required to disqualify from both decisions. (Regulation 18706(b).) 
We have previously advised the City of Sunnyvale that DSP-related decisions that 
implement the DSP, the development plan for Block 18, or the earlier Town Center Project could be 
segmented to allow an official's participation so long as the decisions do not reopen, determine,  
affirm, nullify, or alter the previous decisions approving the DSP. However, we have also advised 
that decisions involving major policy decisions or decisions that may determine whether aspects of 
the DSP will move forward are inextricably interrelated to the decisions in which he has a conflict 
of interest and may not be segmented to allow for his participation.4 
You state that the DSP amendments will be considered first, and then, once the amendments 
are approved, the six specific project decisions will be considered. However, we do not have 
specific decisions to analyze currently. 
As a general matter, we advise that Mayor Klein, Councilmember Larsson and 
Commissioner Rheaume may participate in the specific project decisions to the extent that they do 
not otherwise have a financial interest in the specific project decisions, and the decisions are 
properly segmented from the final DSP amendment decisions under Regulation 18706. Decisions 
that will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of the DSP amendment decisions 
will be inextricably interrelated to the DSP amendment decisions and may not be segmented to 
allow for the official's participation. However, as the nature of any specific decision regarding the 
six specific projects cannot be fully determined prior to the adoption of the DSP amendment, 
considering whether the decisions could be segmented at this time would be premature and purely 
hypothetical. Accordingly, you may wish to seek additional assistance regarding the specific project 
decisions in the event the DSP amendment is adopted.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. May Mayor Klein participate in decisions regarding amendments to the Downtown
Specific Plan ("DSP") where his residence is located within 640 feet of the DSP boundary? 
2. Following the City's decision to amend the DSP, may Councilmember Larsson, Planning
Commissioner Rheaume, or Mayor Klein participate in the decisions relating to the six specific 
development projects within the DSP?

CONCLUSION: 1. No. It is reasonably foreseeable the decisions to amend the DSP will have a material
financial effect on the market value of Mayor Klein's residence under Regulation 18702.2(a)(8),due to the proposed sizable changes in the density, height, and type of use in the DSP 
developments. 
2. Where the officials have a conflict of interest in the DSP amendment decisions, each 
official may only participate in the six specific project decisions to the extent that the official does 
not otherwise have a conflict of interest in the decision, the decision is properly segmented under 
Regulation 18706, and the decision will not in effect reopen, determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the 
DSP amendment decisions. However, until the adoption of the DSP amendment it would premature 
to consider the official's participation the specific project decisions as we cannot identify the exact 
nature of any decision at this time.

Moon Senior Assistant City Attorney 456 West Olive Avenue Sunnyvale, CA 94088-3707 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-19-236 Dear Ms. Moon: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 January 22, 2020 This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Sunnyvale Mayor Larry Klein, Councilmember Gustav Larsson, and Planning Commissioner Ken Rheaume regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. QUESTIONS 1. May Mayor Klein participate in decisions regarding amendments to the Downtown Specific Plan ("DSP") where his residence is located within 640 feet of the DSP boundary? 2. Following the City's decision to amend the DSP, may Councilmember Larsson, Planning Commissioner Rheaume, or Mayor Klein participate in the decisions relating to the six specific development projects within the DSP? CONCLUSIONS 1. No. It is reasonably foreseeable the decisions to amend the DSP will have a material financial effect on the market value of Mayor Klein's residence under Regulation 18702.2(a)(8), 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-19-236 Page No. 2 due to the proposed sizable changes in the density, height, and type of use in the DSP developments. 2. Where the officials have a conflict of interest in the DSP amendment decisions, each official may only participate in the six specific project decisions to the extent that the official does not otherwise have a conflict of interest in the decision, the decision is properly segmented under Regulation 18706, and the decision will not in effect reopen, determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the DSP amendment decisions. However, until the adoption of the DSP amendment it would premature to consider the official's participation the specific project decisions as we cannot identify the exact nature of any decision at this time. FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER Mayor Larry Klein, Councilmember Gustav Larsson, and Planning Commissioner Ken Rheaume each own a single-family home in the residential neighborhood immediately south of the City's Downtown Specific Plan ("DSP") area.
